section.title: Adam and Christ
section.sectionRationale: Paul now lifts his argument to its widest horizon, setting the whole human race under two representative men. Through one man, Adam, sin entered the world and death spread to all, for all sinned in him. But over against Adam stands Christ, the last Adam, whose one act of righteousness brings justification and life to the many. This is the deep structure beneath everything Paul has said about justification: we were counted guilty in Adam our head, and we are counted righteous in Christ our head. The passage insists again and again that the free gift is not like the trespass, for grace superabounds, so that where sin reigned in death, grace now reigns through righteousness to eternal life. This is the theological summit of the letter's first half.
section.thesis: As sin and death came to all through the disobedience of the one man Adam, so righteousness and life come to the many through the obedience of the one man Jesus Christ; and the free gift far outstrips the trespass, so that grace reigns through righteousness to bring eternal life through our Lord.
section.frameNote: Two representative men govern the whole race: Adam, through whom sin and death entered and spread to all; and Christ, whose one act of obedience brings the abounding free gift, so that grace reigns through righteousness to eternal life. The core comparison, the gift far surpassing the trespass, is the climax of the passage.

units.label: Sin and death through one man
units.note: Adam, a pattern of the One to come

verses.text: Therefore, just as sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, so also death was passed on to all men, because all sinned.
For sin was in the world before the law was given; but sin is not taken into account when there is no law.
Nevertheless, death reigned from Adam until Moses, even over those who did not sin in the way that Adam transgressed. He is a pattern of the One to come.

units.label: The free gift is not like the trespass
units.note: grace abounds all the more to the many

verses.text: But the {{charisma|gift}} is not like the trespass. For if the many died by the trespass of the one man, how much more did God’s grace and the gift that came by the grace of the one man, Jesus Christ, abound to the many!
Again, the gift is not like the result of the one man’s sin: The judgment that followed one sin brought condemnation, but the gift that followed many trespasses brought justification.

For if, by the trespass of the one man, death {{basileuo|reigned}} through that one man, how much more will those who receive the abundance of grace and of the gift of righteousness reign in life through the one man, Jesus Christ!

units.label: Grace reigns through righteousness
units.note: one act of obedience, life for the many

verses.text: So then, just as one trespass brought condemnation for all men, so also one act of righteousness brought justification and life for all men.
For just as through the disobedience of the one man the many were made sinners, so also through the {{hypakoe|obedience}} of the one man the many will be made righteous.
The law came in so that the trespass would increase; but where sin increased, {{charis|grace}} increased all the more,
so that, just as sin reigned in death, so also grace might reign through righteousness to bring eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.

charisma.gloss: A free gift, a gift of grace, something bestowed without cost or merit. Paul repeatedly insists the free gift is not like the trespass: whereas Adam's one sin brought condemnation, God's gift in Christ overflows to the many, answering not one sin but many trespasses. The word underlines that righteousness and life come as sheer, undeserved grace, not as anything earned.

basileuo.gloss: To reign, to rule as king. Paul personifies death, sin, and grace as reigning powers. Through Adam, death reigned over the whole race like a tyrant; but those who receive the abundance of grace will themselves reign in life through Christ, and grace reigns through righteousness to eternal life. The gospel is a change of kingdoms and of the power that rules over us.

hypakoe.gloss: Obedience, a hearing that submits. The whole passage pivots on two acts: the disobedience of Adam, by which the many were made sinners, and the obedience of Christ, by which the many will be made righteous. Christ's obedience, His whole life of submission to the Father crowned at the cross, is the one act of righteousness that grounds the believer's justification.

charis.gloss: Grace, God's undeserved favor toward sinners. Paul's climax is that where sin increased, grace increased all the more, superabounding beyond the reach of sin. Grace is not merely a divine attitude but a reigning power that triumphs over sin and death, ruling through righteousness to bring eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.

basic.context: Paul has explained how sinners are justified by faith and given peace with God. Now he steps back to show the biggest picture of all: the whole human race stands under one of two men. Adam sinned, and sin and death spread to everyone. Christ obeyed, and righteousness and life come to all who are His. This is why we could be counted guilty and then counted righteous: it all turns on our representative head.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as a sustained comparison between two men, Adam and Christ, built on the pattern just as, so also. Paul keeps interrupting the comparison to insist it is not an even match: the free gift is far greater than the trespass. Follow the how much more logic. The point is not merely parallel but the overwhelming superiority of grace over sin.
basic.originalLanguages: The passage centers on the charisma, the free gift, which is not like the trespass but overflows. Paul pictures death, sin, and grace as powers that basileuo, reign, like kings. The hinge is two acts: Adam's disobedience and Christ's hypakoe, His obedience. And the summit is charis, grace, that increases all the more where sin increased and reigns to eternal life.
basic.typology: Adam is called a pattern, or type, of the One to come. He is the first head of humanity; Christ is the last Adam, the head of a new humanity. The correspondence is real but reversed: where Adam brought sin, condemnation, and death, Christ brings righteousness, justification, and life. Adam points forward by contrast to the greater Man who undoes his ruin.
basic.christ: Christ is the second and last Adam, the representative Man whose one act of obedience answers Adam's one act of disobedience. His righteousness is credited to the many so that they are made righteous, and through Him grace reigns to eternal life. Where Adam's headship brought death to all in him, Christ's headship brings life to all in Him.
basic.god: God is the giver of the free gift that far outweighs the trespass, the one whose grace superabounds beyond all sin. He is just, for condemnation follows sin, and gracious, for He provides in Christ a righteousness and life the guilty could never earn. The passage magnifies a God whose grace reigns and triumphs.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 15 draws the same Adam and Christ contrast: in Adam all die, in Christ all will be made alive. Genesis 3 records the one man's trespass that Paul builds on. Romans 6 continues the theme of grace reigning without excusing sin. Philippians 2 describes Christ's obedience unto death.
basic.oneStory: The Bible's story runs from one man to one Man. Adam's fall in the garden plunged the whole race into sin and death, and every page since has groaned under that ruin. But God set another Man over a new humanity. Christ's obedience reverses Adam's disobedience, and grace, greater than all our sin, reigns through Him to eternal life.

context.literary: Romans 5:12-21 is a carefully balanced comparison between Adam and Christ, using the just as, so also structure. Paul begins the comparison in verse 12, breaks off in verses 13 to 14 to explain how death reigned before the law, then resumes with a series of contrasts in verses 15 to 19 that stress the surpassing greatness of the gift over the trespass, closing in verses 20 to 21 with grace superabounding and reigning to eternal life. It is the climax of the argument that runs from Romans 1:18.
context.historical: In Jewish thought Adam was widely recognized as the head through whom death came to humanity. Paul takes this shared conviction and sets Christ over against Adam as the head of a redeemed humanity. For a church of Jews and Gentiles, this universal frame shows that all alike stand condemned in Adam and all alike may be justified in Christ, apart from the law which came in later.
context.canonical: This passage, with 1 Corinthians 15:20-49, is the foundation of the doctrine of the two Adams and of federal, or representative, headship. It undergirds the historic understanding of original sin, that all sinned in Adam, and of imputed righteousness, that Christ's obedience is credited to His people. It has been central to Reformed covenant theology.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal the deepest structure of salvation history, that humanity stands under two representative heads, so that believers grasp both the universal ruin brought by Adam and the far greater grace and life brought by Christ, and rest their justification on His obedience rather than their own.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the objective work of the two representative men and its effect on the race. Its abiding truth is doctrinal: all are condemned in Adam and only in Christ, the last Adam, is there justification and life, so that grace, not human works, reigns to eternal life.

debates.question: In what sense did all sin in Adam, so that death spread to all?

views.label: Representative, federal headship
views.case: Adam acted as the covenant head of the race, so that his one sin is imputed to all whom he represents; this best fits Paul's parallel, since Christ's one act of righteousness is likewise credited to those He represents.

views.label: Realistic or seminal union
views.case: Some hold that the whole race was really present in Adam and so participated in his sin, being born with a corrupt nature that issues in death.

debates.bearing: Both affirm that death comes to all through Adam's one sin; the representative reading is foregrounded because it matches the structure of the passage, in which one man's act determines the standing of the many, whether unto condemnation in Adam or justification in Christ.

debates.question: Does one act of righteousness brought justification and life for all men teach that every person is saved?

views.label: All who are in Christ, not every individual
views.case: The historic reading takes the two all men in parallel with the many: as all in Adam are condemned, so all in Christ are justified. Verse 17 limits the gift to those who receive the abundance of grace, so it is not universal salvation but salvation for all who are united to Christ by faith.

views.label: A genuine universalism
views.case: Some press the phrase for all men to mean that Christ's act finally justifies every person without exception.

debates.bearing: The historic reading guards both the greatness of Christ's work and the necessity of receiving it by faith; the gift is offered freely and abounds to the many, but it is received, as verse 17 says, by those who take hold of the abundance of grace.

study.originalLanguages: The passage turns on the charisma, the free gift of grace, which Paul insists is not like the trespass but overflows far beyond it, answering not one sin but many. He personifies death, sin, and grace as powers that basileuo, reign like kings: through Adam death reigned as a tyrant, but the recipients of grace will reign in life through Christ. The hinge of the whole comparison is two contrasting acts, Adam's disobedience and Christ's hypakoe, His obedience, the one act of righteousness that makes the many righteous. And the summit is charis, grace, that increased all the more where sin increased and now reigns through righteousness to bring eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.

typology.type: Adam, a pattern of the One to come
typology.fulfillment: Adam, the first head of humanity, prefigures by contrast Christ the last Adam, the head of a new, redeemed humanity, 1 Corinthians 15:45.
Answered and reversed by the one man's obedience, Christ's whole life of submission crowned at the cross, by which the many are made righteous.
Overturned by the reign of grace through righteousness, so that those in Christ reign in life and inherit eternal life.

study.christ: This passage is one of the clearest windows onto who Christ is and what He has done. He is the last Adam, the representative head of a new humanity, whose single act of obedience answers Adam's single act of disobedience. His righteousness is reckoned to the many so that they are made righteous, not by their own doing but by His. Where Adam's headship spread condemnation and death to all who are in him, Christ's headship pours out justification and life to all who are in Him. And He is the one through whom grace reigns to eternal life: Jesus Christ our Lord, the last word of the passage, is its whole hope.
study.god: The God of this passage is both perfectly just and overwhelmingly gracious. He does not overlook sin, for judgment brings condemnation and death reigns. Yet His response to the flood of human trespass is a greater flood of grace: the free gift is not like the trespass but abounds all the more. Where sin increased, grace superabounded. He is a God whose grace does not merely match sin but triumphs over it, reigning through the righteousness of His Son to bring eternal life, so that all glory belongs to His grace and none to human merit.

crossReferences.note: As in Adam all die, so in Christ all will be made alive; the same two-Adam structure.
The one man's trespass that brought death into the world, the ruin Paul builds his argument upon.
Christ became obedient to death, even death on a cross, the obedience of the one man that makes many righteous.
Sin shall not be your master, because you are not under law but under grace, continuing the reign of grace.

study.oneStory: The whole Bible turns on two men. In Eden one man, Adam, disobeyed, and sin and death spread to the entire race, so that all creation has groaned under the curse. But God appointed another Man, His own Son, the last Adam. By His obedience, crowned at the cross, He undoes Adam's ruin, and grace far greater than all our sin reigns through Him. Where death once ruled as a tyrant, grace now reigns through righteousness to bring eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.

quiz.q: How did sin and death come to the whole human race, according to verse 12?

options.text: Through one man, Adam, sin entered the world, and death spread to all because all sinned.
options.note: Adam is the representative head through whom sin and death came to all.

options.text: Each person's death is caused only by their own separate sins.
options.note: Individual sin is real, but Paul traces death to the one man Adam and the whole race in him.

options.text: Death is simply a natural part of creation with no link to sin.
options.note: Paul says death entered through sin, through the one man's trespass.

options.text: Sin came through the law of Moses.
options.note: Paul says sin was in the world before the law; death reigned from Adam until Moses.

quiz.q: Why does Paul repeat that the free gift is not like the trespass?

options.text: To show that grace in Christ far surpasses and outweighs the ruin brought by Adam.
options.note: The gift answers many trespasses and abounds all the more; grace is greater than sin.

options.text: To say Adam's sin was not really very serious.
options.note: Adam's sin was deadly serious; the point is that grace is even greater.

options.text: To teach that the gift and the trespass are exactly equal.
options.note: Paul stresses the opposite: the gift is not like the trespass but far exceeds it.

options.text: To argue that only a few are affected by Adam's sin.
options.note: The many died by the trespass; the contrast is about the surpassing greatness of grace, not a small scope.

quiz.q: What are the two acts that determine the standing of the many, in verse 19?

options.text: The disobedience of Adam and the obedience of Christ.
options.note: By one man's disobedience the many were made sinners; by the other's obedience made righteous.

options.text: Our own disobedience and our own obedience.
options.note: The passage turns on the acts of the two representative men, not our performance.

options.text: Adam's sin and our keeping of the law.
options.note: Adam's sin is right, but righteousness comes through Christ's obedience, not our law-keeping.

options.text: The obedience of Moses and the obedience of Christ.
options.note: The contrast is Adam and Christ; the law came in later and increased the trespass.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean that Adam is a pattern of the One to come?

options.text: Adam prefigures Christ as a representative head, though by contrast, since Christ reverses Adam's ruin.
options.note: Adam is a type of Christ, the last Adam, head of a new humanity.

options.text: That Adam and Christ were equally sinful men.
options.note: Christ is the obedient one whose righteousness undoes Adam's sin.

options.text: That Christ merely repeats what Adam did.
options.note: Christ does not repeat but reverses Adam's act; the correspondence is by contrast.

options.text: That Adam foreshadows Christ only in appearance, not in role.
options.note: The likeness is precisely in role, one man determining the many, though the outcomes are opposite.

quiz.q: What is the triumphant conclusion in verses 20 and 21?

options.text: Where sin increased, grace increased all the more, reigning through righteousness to eternal life.
options.note: Grace superabounds over sin and reigns to eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.

options.text: Since grace abounds, believers are free to keep on sinning.
options.note: Paul rejects this in Romans 6; grace reigns through righteousness, not license.

options.text: The law finally overcomes sin where grace could not.
options.note: The law increased the trespass; it is grace that superabounds and reigns.

options.text: Sin and grace remain in an equal, endless stalemate.
options.note: Grace does not merely match sin but increases all the more and reigns to eternal life.

questions: Paul says the whole race stands under one of two heads, Adam or Christ. How does understanding that your standing depends on your representative, not your own record, reshape your assurance?
The free gift is not like the trespass; grace far outweighs the ruin. Where do you need to believe that God's grace in Christ is greater than the sin that troubles you most?
Death once reigned as a tyrant, but those who receive grace will reign in life. What would it look like to live today as one who reigns in life through Christ rather than under the fear of death?
Christ's one act of obedience makes the many righteous. How does resting on His obedience, rather than trying to build your own, change the way you approach God?
Where sin increased, grace increased all the more. How can this truth guard you both from despair over your sin and from treating grace as an excuse to sin?

translationNotes.note: Because all sinned renders a phrase debated for centuries: it can mean that all sinned individually or, in context, that all sinned in Adam their representative head. The parallel with Christ, in whom the many are made righteous, favors the representative sense.

A pattern of the One to come renders the word type. Adam is a type of Christ, the correspondence being that one man's act determines the many, though Adam's act and Christ's act have opposite outcomes.

Justification and life for all men stands in parallel with the many of verse 19; read together, all men means all who are in Christ, as verse 17 limits the gift to those who receive the abundance of grace, not to every individual without exception.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 5:12-21. Footnote markers were skipped. No em dashes occurred in this passage. This study is marked as the climax of the letter's first half, with emphasis climax on the core Adam and Christ comparison. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
